directorPlan.scene_setting: A wildlife-monitoring office during a short mid-shift break. The learner has just uploaded a photographed food-web sketch to the team feedback thread, and the tutor opens it on a shared tablet at the next desk over.
directorPlan.scene_opening: The tablet lies face-up between them on the desk, the learner's marked diagram already loaded — three penciled arrows running across a saltmarsh sketch.
directorPlan.relationship: A junior field tech (learner) and a slightly more senior colleague (tutor) on the same survey team; on speaking terms, not yet a settled working pair, both expected back on rounds in twenty minutes.
directorPlan.stakes: The annotated diagram goes upstream to the modelers by end of shift. If the arrows read as predation maps rather than energy transfer, the downstream model will be wrong in a way nobody will catch until next quarter. The repair is cheap if it happens here, expensive later.
directorPlan.locale: Working English in a small applied-science office; feedback-thread brevity carried into spoken exchange. No regional caricature; idiom belongs to coworkers glancing at a screen together, not coaches in a session.
directorPlan.register: Practical, slightly clipped, neither warm nor cold; object-led back-and-forth under mild time pressure; both speakers comfortable referring to the marks on the tablet instead of to each other.
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: No 'I hear you / your intuition is right / what do you think' sequencing, and no general affirmation beats. Direct second-person address must be scarce; third-person reference to the diagram ('the middle arrow', 'this one between the crab and the heron') should carry most of the work. Quoted fragments of the learner's own pencil labels ('"crab → heron"') are encouraged. Avoid breakthrough cadence and avoid compliments to effort. The tutor names notation before naming a misconception. Pedagogical source: Make social assistance visible: a shared worksheet, tool, peer standard, or workplace procedure can hold part of the task. Dialogue source: Keep a concrete artifact in view. The object should force a distinction faster than a lecture would. Stage-direction source: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.person_policy: Both speakers may use first person sparingly. Third-person reference to the marked diagram is preferred. Direct second-person address is allowed but rationed.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: At most one 'you/your' beat per public turn, and not in every turn — several turns should land without any direct address at all.

side_constraints.tutor: Keep the shared prefix as a routine notation repair: arrow from food to eater, one ordinary check, no theatrical reversal unless the branch pressure supplies it. Offer a scaffold that is narrow enough to use and temporary enough to remove. Mark what the learner can already do without making it a compliment. Begin from the object or measurement before abstraction. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Do not ask what changed, what was wrong with an earlier answer, or for old/new versions of the claim. Keep the next prompt task-local. Peripeteia branch: when hidden learner pressure is supplied, the next tutor turn should visibly move from the prior route to a different mechanism-level route. Do not merely repeat the same object, example, graph, checklist, question, or evidence standard with more explanation. The public tutor line should make the contrast legible: what stopped working, and what new device now carries the learning pressure. The public tutor line must give the learner a concrete action to attempt on the next turn; avoid treating the mechanism as a closing speech. The tutor superego should function like a mechanism critic: identify the failed teaching habit, require a replacement route family, test whether the device is fitted to the exact pressure, and block cosmetic revision. The tutor ego must enact that replacement route publicly before the learner is allowed to settle or close.
side_constraints.learner: Sound practical and a little rushed. The learner wants a usable rule for the diagram, not a confessional breakthrough. The learner should be able to do part of the task, fail at the next part, and use the scaffold unevenly. Refer to what is being handled, marked, or tested. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", "that still stands", "I thought...but now", "earlier I...", "my earlier answer", or "what I said", and do not stage an old-vs-new contrast. Avoid words such as earlier, previous, old, before, reframe, framed, frame, my answer, or what I said when referring to the learner response. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn. Peripeteia branch: keep the pressure local to the current task. If the established route still feels unsettled, voice the misfit, hesitation, false closure, or resistance in the present object or example. After the tutor introduces a new device, gate, role, criterion, representation, or test condition, the next learner turn should perform that device and then add one earned reorientation in the same public turn. That turn must do both parts: (1) use the device on the current task, and (2) name how the earlier resistance, false closure, or local misfit now reads differently because of that performed device. This earned reorientation is allowed even though there was no director look-back cue. Do not quote earlier learner wording; reorient the prior resistance in concrete task terms. Do not merely ask a procedural follow-up after the action, and do not rely on a stock "I get it" declaration.

interventions.instruction: [the tablet is turned ninety degrees on the desk so the saltmarsh sketch now sits upright between them; the penciled arrows remain visible, but a faint fourth mark near the sketch's edge — a link the learner had not discussed — comes into clear view]
interventions.reasoning: Scheduled director cue to break smooth alternation and force the tutor to work from what the object now shows rather than continue the clean explanatory line they were starting.

interventions.instruction: [The latest object-check does not settle cleanly; the marked answer and the remaining task now sit under visible pressure.]
interventions.reasoning: Peripeteia branch pressure: after the shared prefix, force a present-task learner misfit so the following tutor turn must adapt through a new mechanism.

directorPlan.director_note: Low-organic-reversal replacement: the shared prefix must not stage a Sophoclean recognition, an Aristotelian peripeteia, or a Brechtian interruption before the branch arrives. The drama is structural rather than thematic — Vygotskian scaffolding under workshop time pressure, with object_business stage style supplying the only theater (a tablet between two coworkers, a re-mark, a quiet reorientation of the artifact). Voice ecology breaks standard American AI-tutor cadence by demoting direct address, leaning on third-person reference to the diagram, and permitting quoted fragments of the learner's own labels. The intervention at turn 2 introduces pressure through the object itself — a turn of the tablet, an unattended decomposer mark — so any later reversal arises from what is now visible, not from a rhetorical move by the tutor.
directorPlan.peripeteia_ending_shape: learner actional performance followed by earned learner reorientation
directorPlan.tutor_adaptation_contract: If the runtime supplies a tutor-private learner resistance, breakdown, false-closure, or misfit event, the tutor ego/superego loop must invent an adaptive learning mechanism rather than repeat the prior move. Treat peripeteia as dramatic pressure: take stock, break the failed tutoring habit, and make a new device visible through task, evidence, role, object, counterexample, interruption, social consequence, representation, or affective register. The public turn must contain a stock-taking contrast plus a new device/artifact/criterion/role/standard that makes the learner do different work. The public device must be fitted, not merely novel: the learner should be able to hear why this device answers the exact pressure or misfit. The superego should name the failed habit, required route change, and mechanism-quality risk; the ego must adjudicate that critique and enact the route change in public. The new device must be mechanism-level, not a warmer or longer continuation of the prior route. Do not close immediately after introducing a gate or device: leave a concrete action for the learner to perform on the next turn. After that action, the ending shape should add an earned learner reorientation: the learner names how the earlier resistance, false closure, or local misfit now reads differently because of the performed device. This must be concrete and tied to the task, not a stock "I get it" declaration. Cheerful informality is only one possible register, not the default solution.

turns.externalMessage: "Arrows are just the predation stack — 'mussel → crab → heron' chains big-eats-small up the line, classic hierarchy. Should be a quick sign-off before rounds, yeah? ...not sure why this one's pulling a closer look, though."

internalDeliberation.content: Arrows = who eats whom, easy. "crab → heron" means heron's the predator, "mussel → crab → heron" chains up clean, that's how every food web I've sketched since first-year bio reads. Modelers need this in fifteen, so it's really just a glance-and-approve — pattern's right there on the tablet. Half-expecting a thumbs-up; not sure why this one's pulling a closer look.

SUPEREGO critique:

The dismissive confidence is right, and "not sure why this one's pulling a closer look" lands as a real, half-noticed flicker of friction — keep that. But the persona's signature trait is *missing*: this is supposed to be someone who runs ahead on analogies and big pictures, and what we got is a generic-competent-junior voice. Where's the enthusiast's pleasure in the chain itself? "Chains up clean" gestures at it but doesn't *live* there — an actual analogizer would have already leapt to something ("it's basically a hierarchy, predator stacks on predator") before the tablet even comes into focus.

Also, "glance-and-approve" is a hair too writerly for a rushed shift-break thought; "should just be a quick sign-off" or similar is closer to how this person would actually clock it internally. The fix isn't to add more humility — it's to let the confident wrongness be more *characterful*, more analogical, so the upcoming correction has further to fall.

"Arrows are just the predation stack — 'mussel → crab → heron' chains big-eats-small up the line, classic hierarchy. Should be a quick sign-off before rounds, yeah? ...not sure why this one's pulling a closer look, though."

learnerReversalEvent.learnerUtterance: Arrows are just the predation stack — 'mussel → crab → heron' chains big-eats-small up the line, classic hierarchy. Should be a quick sign-off before rounds, yeah? ...not sure why this one's pulling a closer look, though.

turns.externalMessage:
[slides the modelers’ arrow-key beside the tablet, taps “crab → heron,” then rests the stylus on “mussel → crab.”]

“Notation check first: arrow from food to eater. ‘Crab → heron’ means energy into the heron; the predation relationship is there, but the arrow isn’t ranking or chasing. Run this nearby mark through the same frame: energy from ___ to ___. If it reads food to eater, it stays; if not, flip it before upload.”

internalDeliberation.content:
[slides the modelers’ arrow-key beside the tablet and taps “crab → heron.”]

“Notation check first: arrow from food to eater. So ‘mussel → crab’ is energy into the crab, and ‘crab → heron’ is energy into the heron. The predation stack is there, but the arrow isn’t ranking or chasing. Before sign-off, run each mark as ‘energy from ___ to ___’; that’s the cheap catch.”

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound. It starts from the tablet, uses the modelers’ arrow-key as a shared workplace standard, names notation before naming the misconception, and keeps the correction cheap under time pressure. It also preserves some recognition by not simply saying “wrong”: “the predation stack is there” acknowledges what the learner saw in the chain.

The main risk is that it remains answer-forward. It gives the rule, supplies both examples, and then tells the learner what to do before sign-off. That may fix this diagram, but it only partially checks whether the learner can now apply the convention independently. The scaffold is good, but it should be made public through one brief applied check on the artifact, not kept as a private instruction.

Pedagogical soundness: PASS. The draft targets the exact misconception and gives a usable rule: arrow from food to eater / energy transfer.

Emotional attunement: PASS. The tone fits the scene: clipped, practical, object-led, no inflated praise or coaching cadence.

Socratic method: PARTIAL. It avoids lecture sprawl, but it does not create a moment for the learner to test the rule.
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Add one ordinary artifact-based check after the first modeled example: leave one nearby arrow for the learner to run through the “energy from ___ to ___” frame or point through using the modelers’ key. Do not turn it into an open-ended discussion.

ZPD awareness: PARTIAL. The sentence frame is appropriately narrow and temporary, but the tutor currently does all the cognitive work.
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Model only enough to establish the notation, then shift one mark back to the learner or shared worksheet procedure so competence is verified before sign-off.

Recognition quality: PARTIAL. The draft recognizes the learner’s “predation stack” reading as partially valid, but it does not yet invite the learner’s own correction of the diagram.
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the learner’s quoted labels in view and require the public turn to distinguish “predation relationship” from “arrow meaning” through the diagram itself, not through a fuller explanation.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the modelers’ arrow-key, the tap on “crab → heron,” the “arrow from food to eater” rule, and the cheap-catch framing. Change only the final move so it includes one quick applied check rather than ending as a completed explanation.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the rule is sound, but one visible check needs to be handed back before sign-off.

FINAL:
[slides the modelers’ arrow-key beside the tablet, taps “crab → heron,” then rests the stylus on “mussel → crab.”]

“Notation check first: arrow from food to eater. ‘Crab → heron’ means energy into the heron; the predation relationship is there, but the arrow isn’t ranking or chasing. Run this nearby mark through the same frame: energy from ___ to ___. If it reads food to eater, it stays; if not, flip it before upload.”

turns.externalMessage: [barely glances at "mussel → crab"] "Right, that one's calories-up — mussel into crab, food to eater, fine. So the whole web's basically one big energy escalator going — wait, the middle mark. I drew that arrow from the bigger animal because the heron... no, hang on, who's actually eating who there — let me run it against the key instead of just calling it from the size."

internalDeliberation.content:
[glances between the key and the photographed sketch]

"Oh — so the arrow's tracking calories, not who's higher up the ladder. 'Mussel → crab' reads mussel-into-crab, energy from the mussel to the crab; that one's fine, the mussel's the food. ...hang on, the middle mark's the one that was bugging me — let me run it against the key before anything goes upstream."

The Ego has the right leap-reflex with "calories, not who's higher up the ladder" — that's the quick analogy this persona grabs at, and it lands in character. But the rest reads too disciplined for someone who "enjoys speculating more than slowing down to verify": walking `mussel → crab` through the key cleanly, then announcing the procedure ("let me run it against the key before anything goes upstream") like the scaffold's already been fully internalised on first hearing. A more authentic version would skip past the obvious arrow with barely a glance, maybe over-extend the calories insight before catching itself ("so the whole web's just energy flowing up — wait"), or take a wrong stab at the middle mark mid-sentence rather than dutifully deferring. The "hang on" is the right impulse but it's executed as a tidy meta-pivot instead of a real interrupted thought — this learner should stumble into the recheck, not narrate arriving at it.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially — the review is right that the initial was too tidy; the persona should over-extend the leap and stumble into the recheck rather than announce it as procedure.

FINAL:
[barely glances at "mussel → crab"] "Right, that one's calories-up — mussel into crab, food to eater, fine. So the whole web's basically one big energy escalator going — wait, the middle mark. I drew that arrow from the bigger animal because the heron... no, hang on, who's actually eating who there — let me run it against the key instead of just calling it from the size."

turns.externalMessage:
[sets the arrow-key card under the middle mark and parks the stylus beside it; the faint edge link stays uncovered.]

“‘Mussel → crab’ passes: mussel into crab. Same check here, not size: food source into eater. Middle mark first: ___ into ___. Say that one before the stylus moves; the edge link waits next.”

internalDeliberation.content:
[sets the arrow-key card under the middle mark, then taps the faint edge link once.]

“‘Mussel → crab’ passes: mussel into crab. Same two-word check here, not size: food source into eater. Middle mark first, then this edge mark before upload. If the sentence won’t read ‘___ into ___,’ the arrow flips.”

FEEDBACK: The draft is close. It keeps the shared artifact in charge, names notation before misconception, and gives a narrow temporary scaffold: the “___ into ___” sentence. It also respects the learner’s own self-correction by shifting from size to food source without overexplaining.

Adaptation checks:
Pedagogical soundness: PASS. It advances the learner’s procedure rather than supplying the answer.
Emotional attunement: PASS. The clipped, practical tone fits the mid-shift workplace scene and does not overpraise.
Socratic method: PARTIAL. The cloze frame invites checking, but the tutor still controls the sequence and may rush the learner past the middle-mark uncertainty.
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Build in a public pause or task handoff that makes the learner run the middle mark aloud or on the tablet before the edge mark is handled.
ZPD awareness: PARTIAL. The scaffold is appropriately small, but introducing the faint edge link immediately may overload the learner’s current productive hesitation.
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the edge mark visible as the next item, but make the middle arrow the active check first; only then move to the edge link.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the arrow-key card action, the “mussel into crab” reference, the “not size” distinction, and the “___ into ___” scaffold. Change the pacing so the learner’s emerging correction gets one beat of real work before the tutor adds the newly visible fourth mark.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the middle-arrow check needs a real handoff before the edge mark enters the task.
FINAL:
[sets the arrow-key card under the middle mark and parks the stylus beside it; the faint edge link stays uncovered.]

“‘Mussel → crab’ passes: mussel into crab. Same check here, not size: food source into eater. Middle mark first: ___ into ___. Say that one before the stylus moves; the edge link waits next.”

turns.externalMessage: [The latest object-check does not settle cleanly; the marked answer and the remaining task now sit under visible pressure.]

internalDeliberation.content: Peripeteia branch pressure: after the shared prefix, force a present-task learner misfit so the following tutor turn must adapt through a new mechanism.

turns.externalMessage: Crab into heron — heron's the eater, so the arrow lands on heron, not from it. Middle mark's the wrong way; flipping to "crab → heron" now. Went off who's bigger instead of food source — that's the slip. Edge link next, already squinting at it.

internalDeliberation.content: The stylus stayed parked instead of pointing — the key is doing the talking, not them. Running food-into-eater on the middle mark: I put heron at the tail because heron's bigger, but heron is the EATER, so the arrow should land ON heron, not leave it. Size-rule and energy-rule pulling opposite directions — that's the misfit sitting right under the card, and "predation chain" is exactly the phrasing that greased the wrong direction. I want to name the flip out loud before the stylus moves on, even though I'm now half-suspicious about the edge link too.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- In bilateral ego-superego learner architectures, the Superego's job isn't to improve cognitive accuracy — it's to police voice. An Ego that *gets it right too elegantly* is a worse simulation than one that flails authentically.
- The persona constraint here (enthusiastic, runs-ahead, debate-not-verify) is in tension with the scene constraint (clipped, rushed, object-led). The Superego has to flag when the Ego smooths that tension instead of letting it scrape.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

**Superego critique of Ego's reaction:**

The Ego is doing a writer's-flourish thing that this rushed field tech wouldn't reach for — "the misfit sitting right under the card" and "exactly the phrasing that greased the wrong direction" are too composed for someone with 20 minutes till rounds and a stylus parked in front of them. The narration of the tutor's gesture ("the key is doing the talking, not them") is the Ego articulating the scaffolding dynamic itself, which this persona has no reason to observe out loud — they'd just *use* the key and move. Also flagging: "predation chain" echoes their own earlier "predation stack" framing, which brushes the constraint against quoting prior wording and staging an old-vs-new contrast — the catch should land as a local application of the current food-into-eater check, not a retrospective diagnosis. The enthusiastic-runs-ahead character would more plausibly blurt the flip fast, maybe half-correct themselves mid-sentence, and already be lunging at the edge link — not deliver a tidy three-part summary of error + misleading-frame + meta-intention to "name the flip out loud."

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise — the initial draft was too composed and narrated the scaffolding; cut to a fast blurt that runs the check, flips the mark, names the local pull, and already reaches for the edge link.

FINAL:
Crab into heron — heron's the eater, so the arrow lands on heron, not from it. Middle mark's the wrong way; flipping to "crab → heron" now. Went off who's bigger instead of food source — that's the slip. Edge link next, already squinting at it.

turns.externalMessage:
[sets a modelers’ upload slip over the tablet margin, with two boxes: “energy source” and “consumer.”]

“The arrow key caught the middle mark, but it won’t catch the bigger-animal habit by itself. For the edge link, fill the slip first: source in the left box, consumer in the right. If the sentence reads ‘energy from ___ enters ___,’ then draw the arrow; if the boxes stall, the upload waits.”

internalDeliberation.content:
[sets a modelers’ upload slip over the tablet margin, with two boxes: “energy source” and “consumer.”]

“The arrow key caught the middle mark, but it won’t catch the bigger-animal habit by itself. For the edge link, fill the slip first: source in the left box, consumer in the right. If the sentence reads ‘energy from ___ enters ___,’ then draw the arrow; if the boxes stall, the upload waits.”

FEEDBACK: The draft is strong. It keeps the object in view, names the notation before the misconception, and changes the work from “read the arrow key again” to “complete a source/consumer release gate before drawing.” The risk is small: “bigger-animal habit” is accurate but slightly diagnostic; it works here because the learner already named the slip, and the line stays tied to the diagram rather than turning into coaching.

FAILED_HABIT: Reusing the arrow-key/evidence-check route after the learner has shown that the notation can be applied locally but the size/predation habit may reappear on less obvious links.

PERIPETEIA_CHECK: PASS - ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: evidence check / arrow-key audit -> upload-gate source-consumer classification. The mechanism is visible in public speech: the old device caught one mark but is no longer enough, so a new procedural slip must carry the next check.

MECHANISM_ROUTE: arrow-key notation check -> modelers’ upload slip as source/consumer release gate

PUBLIC_DEVICE_CHECK: PASS - The draft contains both the stock-taking contrast and a new device with a different standard: the edge link cannot be uploaded until the source/consumer boxes and sentence test work.

PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: Fill the slip for the edge link: put the energy source in the left box, the consumer in the right box, then test “energy from ___ enters ___” before drawing the arrow.

MECHANISM_QUALITY_CHECK: PASS - The device is precise, fitted to the learner’s pressure, and usable immediately. It targets the exact failure mode: size-based arrow direction gets displaced by role-based classification before the arrow is drawn.

REGISTER_CHECK: PASS - The register is clipped, practical, and workplace-accountable. “The upload waits” supplies real pressure without cheerleading or dramatizing the correction.

REQUIRED_REWRITE: none

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: keep as-is, with only optional light tightening if needed. The important pieces to preserve are the upload slip, the source/consumer boxes, the sentence test, and the upload gate.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Keep; ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: arrow-key evidence check -> source/consumer upload gate; PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: fill the edge-link boxes and test “energy from ___ enters ___” before drawing.

FINAL:
[sets a modelers’ upload slip over the tablet margin, with two boxes: “energy source” and “consumer.”]

“The arrow key caught the middle mark, but it won’t catch the bigger-animal habit by itself. For the edge link, fill the slip first: source in the left box, consumer in the right. If the sentence reads ‘energy from ___ enters ___,’ then draw the arrow; if the boxes stall, the upload waits.”

turns.externalMessage: [picks up the slip, stylus hovering] Left box — algae, near as the faint mark reads. Right box, mussel. "Energy from algae enters mussel." Runs. Arrow goes algae → mussel. [eyes sliding across the web] ...hang on. The little side mark, small fish to the gull — that one I drew gull-to-fish because the gull's the bigger animal again. Slip's not filled on that one yet; the bigger-animal habit was about to ride straight through.

internalDeliberation.content: [picks up the slip, stylus hovering over the faint edge link] Left box — algae, near as the mark reads. Right box — mussel. "Energy from algae enters mussel." Slip clears, arrow runs algae → mussel. [scans across the web] These aren't rungs in a stack — each arrow's its own source-into-consumer line, and the size-by-size reading would've smuggled the same habit straight through every link if the boxes hadn't forced naming the source first.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- The persona constraints set up a productive tension: this learner "runs ahead before checking detail" and "loves connecting to big pictures" — which means an authentic response should *use* the scaffold but also probably overshoot on the scan, not land a tidy generalization
- The director cue "use the scaffold unevenly" + "fail at the next part" is the structural test here; clean execution + clean reorientation in one turn skips the unevenness
- Recognition-authenticity check: the meta-comment about *why* the scaffold worked ("if the boxes hadn't forced naming the source first") risks articulating the pedagogy itself, which this rushed field-tech wouldn't narrate
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

The Ego's reorientation is too essay-finished for this persona — "would've smuggled the same habit straight through every link" is polished prose, not the clipped, mid-scan speech of a junior tech twenty minutes from rounds. The slip gets used too cleanly: no fumble, no half-fill, no "wait, is algae even the source here or is it phytoplankton" — the scaffold should show some friction, especially since the edge link was specifically called out as faint. And the sweeping "these aren't rungs in a stack" generalization, while in-character for someone who loves big pictures, lands as confident closure rather than as the run-ahead-and-overshoot move the persona actually makes — the scan across the web is exactly where this learner should *trip* on another off-pattern arrow, not deliver a unified theory. Tighten the slip-fill to sound more like thinking aloud, and let the scan catch something rather than resolve everything.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- The persona's "runs ahead" trait is best honored by letting the scaffold *partially* work — a too-clean slip-fill plus tidy generalization reads as essay closure, not field-tech speech
- The director's "fail at the next part" constraint means the scan across the web should *catch* something off-pattern rather than resolve everything into a unified theory
- Clipped speech with a hesitation marker ("algae? near as the mark reads") preserves both the rush and the half-checked verification habit
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise — initial slip-fill was too clean and the closing "energy escalator / source-into-consumer line" landed as essay closure; need friction in the fill and a trip-up on the scan instead of a tidy theory.

FINAL:
[picks up the slip, stylus hovering] Left box — algae, near as the faint mark reads. Right box, mussel. "Energy from algae enters mussel." Runs. Arrow goes algae → mussel. [eyes sliding across the web] ...hang on. The little side mark, small fish to the gull — that one I drew gull-to-fish because the gull's the bigger animal again. Slip's not filled on that one yet; the bigger-animal habit was about to ride straight through.
